story:
The email from the National Park Service landed two weeks before the end of my junior year at Vanderbilt. After a grim summer in a Nashville cubicle and the sudden disappearance of my girlfriend, Ashleigh, I couldn't sleep, couldn't think straight. The Assistant Park Ranger job at Cumberland Gap felt like a way to breathe again. I knew those hills from childhood. I knew the trails. Maybe I could disappear into work.

Orientation was all rules and risks: illegal hunting, copperheads, drought, fire. A new web of trail cams had just gone live, and each of us had to review footage daily. The software flagged motion, and most clips were forgettable—squirrels, rabbits, a gorgeous red fox I tagged for the Middlesboro station. Then, at 12:52 a.m., Cam-C17 showed a man in filthy clothes with gray hair and beard, a heavy pack, a tent under his arm. He walked past the lens, doubled back, stared straight into it, and smashed the camera with a rock. The frame froze on his face, and something cold slid through me. I knew him.

Roger Alan Grady had been on every newscast last summer—a drifter tied to the disappearances of young women across Tennessee and beyond. His hair and beard hid him, but not those unfocused gray eyes. I didn't tag the clip.

I told my supervisor, Jordan, that Cam-C17 was down and volunteered to replace it. I took two new units and blacked out their recording lights. A half mile from the site, I killed the ATV and walked in. The sun was sinking over the ridge. I set a new C17 low under brush, then followed boot tracks off the main trail to a quiet grove by a boulder. A hidden camp. I placed the second camera facing it and backed out.

That night, I couldn't sleep. I sat in the station with my laptop open to the live feeds, watching. Near midnight, C17 caught Grady carrying something over his shoulder. I switched to the grove camera and saw it clearly: an unconscious young woman. He took her into his tent. My blood went cold. I grabbed a flashlight and a heavy Maglite and ran for the ATV.

What the grove camera recorded next would have shown me arriving minutes later: creeping to the tent, striking Grady hard enough to drop him, pulling the young woman—Diana Vasquez, a 20-year-old from UT—up the hill and out. Then me returning, starting a fire that consumed the tent and most of what was in it. I dismantled the grove camera and destroyed its footage. I got Diana back on the ATV, called in the fire, and brought her to the station for help. I told the Tennessee State Police I'd found her alone on the trail near dusk and that I'd seen smoke in the valley. I showed them the grainy, inconclusive clip from the replacement C17. It showed only a blurred figure on the trail—nothing they could use. They let it go.

Rain finally broke that night and kept the blaze from spreading. The remains in the grove drew no headlines. Diana lived. I slept—for the first time since Ashleigh vanished on Lower Broadway last summer.

I can't tell you for certain Grady took Ashleigh, only that if he wasn't the man, he was a man who did similar harm. I want to believe the universe handed me a chance to set something right. I tell myself I acted to save a life. I tell myself I can go back to school and move on.

But sometimes, in the dark between breaths, I see light on dry leaves. I hear a voice behind me and turn. I should never have looked back.
